children.name: Vipera
children.description: True vipers of the Palearctic — stocky, cryptically patterned snakes with the classic triangular head that warns of their solenoglyphous (hollow front-fang) venom delivery system. The Common Adder is the only venomous reptile native to Scandinavia and Britain; its range extends further north than any other snake, reaching the Arctic Circle in Norway. Vipera species are predominantly viviparous — they retain eggs internally and give birth to live young, an adaptation that allows females to thermoregulate the developing embryos by basking.

children.name: Vipera berus
children.description: The only venomous snake in Britain and Scandinavia. The distinctive dark zigzag dorsal stripe is the classic field mark. Bites are rarely fatal to healthy adults; fewer than 15 deaths recorded in the UK in the last century. Gives birth to live young in late summer.

children.name: Vipera aspis
children.description: Common across southern Europe from the Pyrenees to the Balkans. Thought to be the asp used by Cleopatra in her suicide, though the Egyptian Cobra is a more probable candidate. Venom is more potent than the Common Adder's.

children.name: Vipera ammodytes
children.description: The most venomous European viper, with a distinctive fleshy horn on the snout. Found in rocky, dry habitats across the Balkans and into Asia Minor. Its potent venom requires antivenom treatment in most bite cases.

children.name: Vipera ursinii
children.description: Europe's smallest viper, inhabiting alpine meadows and steppe grasslands. Classified as Vulnerable; highly fragmented populations are threatened by habitat loss, tourism, and illegal collection for the pet trade.

children.name: Vipera latastei
children.description: The only Vipera species found on the Iberian Peninsula and northwestern Africa, bearing a distinctive soft horn on the snout similar to V. ammodytes. It favours dry, rocky hillsides with scrub vegetation in Spain, Portugal, and Morocco. Listed as Vulnerable due to habitat degradation and fragmented population structure.

children.name: Vipera seoanei
children.description: Restricted to the humid Atlantic coast of the Iberian Peninsula and southwestern France, where it occupies heathlands, meadows, and forest margins. Unlike most Vipera, it tolerates wetter habitats. Polymorphic in colour from grey to reddish-brown with a classic dorsal zigzag.

children.name: Vipera xanthina
children.description: A large, robustly built viper of Turkey, Greece, and the northern Levant, reaching up to 95 cm. It inhabits rocky slopes, scrubland, and agricultural margins up to 2 500 m elevation. Its venom is medically significant and bites are not uncommon in rural Anatolia.

children.name: Vipera kaznakovi
children.description: A brilliantly coloured viper of the western Caucasus, with males displaying vivid orange-red and black dorsal patterns. Found in montane forest edges and subalpine meadows in Georgia and northeastern Turkey. Endangered due to its restricted range and collection for the illegal pet trade.

children.name: Vipera renardi
children.description: Inhabits the open steppes and semi-deserts from eastern Europe to Central Asia, where it is one of the most abundant vipers. Tolerant of cold, it remains active at low temperatures and is one of the last snakes to enter hibernation. Formerly lumped with V. ursinii but now recognised as a distinct species.

children.name: Vipera ebneri
children.description: A little-known steppe viper from Kazakhstan and adjacent Central Asia, closely related to V. renardi. It lives in arid grasslands and semi-desert scrub, feeding primarily on lizards and small rodents. Taxonomy within this species complex remains under active revision.

children.name: Vipera olguni
children.description: A recently described mountain viper from northeastern Turkey; limited to alpine meadows above 2000 m.

children.name: Vipera dahlseni
children.description: A poorly known viper from Turkey and Iran; high elevation specialist.

children.name: Vipera monticola
children.description: A small high-altitude viper from the Atlas Mountains of Morocco; rarely exceeds 35 cm.

children.name: Vipera latifii
children.description: A critically endangered viper from a single valley in Iran's Alborz Mountains.

children.name: Vipera transvera
children.description: A Balkan species with distinctive transverse dorsal bars.

children.name: Montivipera
children.description: Mountain vipers of the Middle East and Caucasus — large, robust vipers restricted to high-elevation rocky habitats in Turkey, Iran, and adjacent countries. Formerly included in Vipera, Montivipera was elevated to genus status based on molecular phylogenetics. Many species are listed as Vulnerable or Endangered due to their narrow ranges, slow reproductive rates, and ongoing collection for the illegal pet trade.

children.name: Montivipera xanthina
children.description: One of the largest vipers in the Middle East, reaching up to 95 cm, found across Turkey and the northeastern Aegean islands. It inhabits rocky slopes, sparse woodland margins, and terraced agricultural land up to 2 500 m. Its medically significant venom causes local necrosis and systemic effects.

children.name: Montivipera raddei
children.description: A large, heavily built mountain viper of northeastern Turkey, Armenia, and northwestern Iran, typically found at 1 500–3 000 m. Its venom is potent and bites in remote mountain communities can be life-threatening. Named after the naturalist Gustav Radde.

children.name: Montivipera wagneri
children.description: Critically Endangered and among the rarest snakes in the world, known from a single small area straddling the Iran–Turkey border at high altitude. Striking orange-red and black colouration has made it a prized target for illegal collectors. Fewer than 250 mature individuals are estimated to remain.

children.name: Montivipera albizona
children.description: Endemic to a small area of the Taurus Mountains in south-central Turkey, living at 1 800–2 500 m elevation. Distinguished by a distinctive pale dorsal crossband pattern unlike other Montivipera. Vulnerable due to its very restricted range and exposure to collection pressure.

children.name: Montivipera latifi
children.description: A critically endangered viper from the Elburz Mountains of Iran; one of the rarest vipers.

children.name: Montivipera bornmuelleri
children.description: From the mountains of Lebanon and Israel; threatened by habitat loss.

children.name: Macrovipera
children.description: Large vipers of the Mediterranean and Middle East — among the largest Old World vipers, reaching over 2 m in the case of Macrovipera lebetina. These heavily built snakes inhabit dry, rocky terrain from Cyprus and the Levant to Central Asia. Their venom is highly potent and envenomations cause severe local necrosis, coagulopathy, and systemic haemorrhage. Several island-endemic subspecies are considered Critically Endangered.

children.name: Macrovipera lebetina
children.description: The largest viper in the Middle East and one of the most medically important, with a broad range from Cyprus and the Levant across Central Asia. Island populations on Cyprus and the Milos archipelago are genetically distinct. Responsible for significant snakebite morbidity in Turkey, Iran, and Pakistan.

children.name: Macrovipera schweizeri
children.description: A Critically Endangered island endemic restricted to four small islands in the Milos archipelago of the Greek Cyclades. Population is estimated at fewer than 2 500 adults. Threatened by illegal collection for the herpetological trade and habitat degradation by goat grazing.

children.name: Daboia
children.description: Russell's Viper — one of the four 'Big Four' venomous snakes of South Asia responsible for the vast majority of snakebite deaths and disability on the subcontinent. Its venom causes a complex syndrome involving haemorrhage, kidney failure, and coagulation disorders. Found in open, cultivated lands across South and Southeast Asia, and frequently encountered by agricultural workers. Its Burmese name, 'chain viper', refers to its distinctive chain-like dorsal pattern.

children.name: Daboia russelii
children.description: Estimated to cause 30,000–40,000 deaths annually in South Asia. The leading cause of acute kidney failure from snakebite globally. Loud, prolonged hiss serves as a warning before striking.

children.name: Daboia siamensis
children.description: Formerly treated as a subspecies of D. russelii, the Eastern Russell's Viper ranges across Myanmar, Thailand, southern China, and Indonesia. Its venom composition differs notably from the Indian species and causes a distinct clinical syndrome including severe coagulopathy. A major cause of snakebite mortality in Myanmar and Indochina.

children.name: Bitis
children.description: African adders — heavily built African vipers including the Puff Adder, the most medically important snake in Africa, and the spectacular Gaboon Viper with the longest fangs of any snake. Bitis species are ambush predators, lying motionless for days in leaf litter or on rocky ground until prey passes within range. The Puff Adder is responsible for more snakebite deaths and morbidity in Africa than any other species — partly because it freezes rather than fleeing, making it easy to step on.

children.name: Bitis gabonica
children.description: Has the longest fangs of any venomous snake — up to 5 cm — and the highest venom yield per bite. Despite its size (up to 2 m), its geometric brown-and-purple pattern renders it almost invisible in fallen leaves. Rarely bites unless stepped on.

children.name: Bitis arietans
children.description: Responsible for more snakebite fatalities in Africa than any other species. Named for its threat display — inflating the body and exhaling loudly ('puffing'). Widely distributed from Morocco to Arabia; absent only from rainforest and true desert.

children.name: Bitis nasicornis
children.description: One of Africa's most ornate snakes, adorned with a vivid mosaic of blue, purple, crimson, and yellow geometric patches and a distinctive cluster of 2–3 horn-like scales on the snout. A rainforest species found from West Africa to the Congo Basin, usually near permanent water. Its venom is highly potent and bites, though rare, are considered medical emergencies.

children.name: Bitis caudalis
children.description: A small desert-adapted viper of the Namib and Kalahari with a single horn-like scale over each eye used to break up its outline against sand. Moves by sidewinding across loose sand, leaving distinctive J-shaped tracks. Preys primarily on lizards which it ambushes by burying itself in sand with only its eyes and tail tip visible.

children.name: Bitis peringueyi
children.description: One of the world's smallest vipers, rarely exceeding 30 cm, perfectly adapted to the Namib Desert sand dunes. Eyes positioned on the top of the head allow the snake to observe prey while fully buried in loose sand. Its caudal luring — wiggling the tail tip — attracts sand lizards within striking range.

children.name: Bitis atropos
children.description: A stocky mountain adder endemic to South Africa and Lesotho, found in fynbos, montane grassland, and rocky outcrops at elevations up to 3 000 m. Unusually for Bitis, its venom contains a significant neurotoxic component affecting vision and causing ptosis and diplopia in envenomed patients.

children.name: Bitis schneideri
children.description: A small, richly patterned Bitis from the Namib Desert; sidewinding locomotion on sand.

children.name: Bitis worthingtoni
children.description: A highland species from the Kenyan Rift Valley; one of the least-known Bitis.

children.name: Bitis heraldica
children.description: Endemic to Angola's central highlands; distinguished by its unique scale colouration.

children.name: Bitis inornata
children.description: A recently described species from South Africa; drab colouration, no pattern.

children.name: Bitis albanica
children.description: A small adder restricted to the Eastern Cape, South Africa; grey with dark zigzag.

children.name: Bitis heraldi
children.description: A small Angolan adder with a pattern of dark saddles on a tan background.

children.name: Cerastes
children.description: Horned desert vipers of North Africa and the Middle East — superbly adapted to life in erg sand deserts and rocky hammada. Cerastes move by sidewinding, a locomotion mode that minimises contact with superheated sand. The iconic horns over the eyes, present in most individuals of C. cerastes, are individually variable; some lack them entirely. They bury themselves in sand using lateral undulations to vanish in seconds, waiting in ambush for lizards and small rodents.

children.name: Cerastes cerastes
children.description: The iconic horned viper of the Sahara Desert, found from Morocco east to Arabia. The supraocular horns are variably expressed — some populations lack them entirely. A master of sand burial, it disappears below the surface within seconds using lateral wriggling motions, leaving only its eyes and horn tips exposed.

children.name: Cerastes vipera
children.description: A hornless, smaller counterpart to C. cerastes, found across the Sahara from Morocco to the Sinai. Despite the lack of horns, it is equally adapted to erg deserts and sidewinds efficiently across soft sand. Feeds almost exclusively on small lizards and occasionally small rodents.

children.name: Causus
children.description: Night adders of sub-Saharan Africa — a unique genus of vipers that retain several plesiomorphic (ancestral) features, including large, plate-like head scales unlike the small fragmented scales typical of vipers. Causus are relatively small, primarily toad-eating vipers that are broadly adapted to a wide range of African habitats. Their venom is less potent than most other vipers and bites are rarely life-threatening, though painful local reactions are common.

children.name: Causus rhombeatus
children.description: The most widespread night adder, found across sub-Saharan Africa in a variety of habitats from savanna to forest edge. Almost exclusively a toad specialist — its expandable stomach can accommodate toads equal to its own body diameter. Terrestrial and crepuscular; often found near water.

children.name: Causus maculatus
children.description: Ranges across West and Central Africa in moist savanna and forest-savanna mosaic habitats. Like its relatives, it feeds primarily on anurans — frogs as well as toads. Relatively common in agricultural landscapes and semi-urban areas where its toad prey is abundant.

children.name: Causus bilineatus
children.description: Distinguished from other night adders by a pair of pale dorsolateral stripes running the length of the body. Found across central Africa from Angola and the DRC east to Tanzania. A forest and forest-edge species with poorly documented ecology.

children.name: Echis
children.description: Saw-scaled vipers — small but extraordinarily dangerous vipers of Africa, the Middle East, and South Asia. Echis species are responsible for more snakebite fatalities worldwide than any other genus of venomous snake — a consequence of their abundance, irascible temperament, and the high human population density in their range. Their common name derives from the threat display: rubbing serrated lateral scales together to produce a distinctive rasping warning sound, conserving venom by warning before striking.

children.name: Echis carinatus
children.description: One of the 'Big Four' most dangerous snakes of the Indian subcontinent, responsible for enormous snakebite mortality. Small (rarely over 60 cm) but aggressive; strikes repeatedly and quickly when threatened. Occurs in sandy and rocky dry habitats from West Africa to Sri Lanka.

children.name: Echis ocellatus
children.description: The dominant Echis species across the West African Sahel, responsible for a staggering burden of snakebite morbidity and mortality in Nigeria, Burkina Faso, Ghana, and neighbouring countries. Described as causing up to 20 000 deaths annually in West Africa alone. Antivenom supply in the region remains critically inadequate.

children.name: Echis coloratus
children.description: The largest Echis species, reaching up to 75 cm, found in rocky desert and semi-desert habitats from Egypt and Israel through the Arabian Peninsula. Unlike most saw-scaled vipers, it prefers rocky hillsides over sand. Its venom has a different composition from E. carinatus with pronounced haemotoxic effects.

children.name: Echis pyramidum
children.description: Widespread in northeastern Africa from Libya and Egypt to Kenya and Ethiopia, extending into the Arabian Peninsula. Occupies sandy and rocky semi-arid habitats. The species complex has been split into multiple taxa in recent revisions; its taxonomy remains an active area of study.

children.name: Echis leakeyi

children.name: Atheris
children.description: African bush vipers — a genus of small, arboreal vipers restricted to the rainforests and montane forests of equatorial Africa. Most species are vividly coloured in greens and yellows, providing camouflage in forest vegetation. Their strongly prehensile tails and laterally compressed bodies are adaptations for life in the canopy. Bush vipers are rarely encountered and few bites are recorded, but their venom is potently haemotoxic and several deaths have been documented in the absence of antivenom.

children.name: Atheris squamigera
children.description: The most widespread and best-known bush viper, found across the Congo Basin and West Africa in lowland and montane rainforest. Vivid green with a heavily keeled scale texture giving a rough, spiky appearance. Highly arboreal; rarely descends to the ground. Preys on small mammals, frogs, and lizards.

children.name: Atheris chlorechis
children.description: Found in the coastal rainforests of West Africa from Guinea to Ghana. Bright green with a yellow-tipped tail used for caudal luring. Less commonly encountered than A. squamigera due to its more restricted rainforest habitat. Its venom causes coagulopathy and severe local effects.

children.name: Atheris nitschei
children.description: A montane bush viper of the Albertine Rift, found in Uganda, Rwanda, Burundi, DRC, and Tanzania at elevations of 1 500–2 500 m. Darker green to olive, contrasting with the vivid green lowland species. One of the few bush vipers for which clinical envenomation data have been published.

children.name: Atheris hispida
children.description: Arguably the most striking Atheris species, with elongated, strongly keeled scales that give the body a bristly, almost otherworldly appearance. Found in lowland and montane rainforest of Uganda, DRC, and Kenya. Highly arboreal; individuals perch head-down on branches awaiting passing prey.

children.name: Atheris rungweensis

One of the least-studied Atheris; its ecology and venom composition remain poorly characterised. Listed as Vulnerable due to its restricted montane habitat.

children.name: Crotalus
children.description: Rattlesnakes — pit vipers of North and South America with a unique interlocking keratin rattle at the tail tip. Each time the snake sheds its skin, a new rattle segment is added — though segments can break off, so the number does not reliably indicate age. The rattle is a warning device, deployed when the snake is threatened, not a hunting tool. Rattlesnakes detect warm-blooded prey using heat-sensitive pit organs between the eye and nostril — infrared imaging that is effective in total darkness.

children.name: Crotalus atrox
children.description: The snake responsible for more snakebite fatalities in North America than any other species. Venom is primarily hemotoxic, destroying tissue and inhibiting clotting. Iconic and culturally significant across the American Southwest.

children.name: Crotalus horridus
children.description: Once ranged across most of eastern North America. Now restricted to fragmented woodland populations; classified as threatened or endangered in many northeastern states. Historically revered — appearing on the Gadsden Flag ('Don't Tread on Me').

children.name: Crotalus scutulatus
children.description: Often considered the most dangerous rattlesnake in North America due to its unusually potent neurotoxic 'Mojave A' venom, which can cause respiratory failure. Two venom types occur — type A (neurotoxic) and type B (hemotoxic).

children.name: Crotalus adamanteus
children.description: The largest rattlesnake in the world, reaching up to 2.4 m and weighing over 10 kg. Found in the longleaf pine savannas and palmetto flatwoods of the southeastern United States. Populations have declined sharply due to habitat loss; it has been extirpated from Louisiana and appears rarely in other peripheral states.

children.name: Crotalus viridis
children.description: The most widespread rattlesnake in North America, ranging from southern Canada to northern Mexico across the Great Plains and Rocky Mountain foothills. Communal hibernation dens (hibernacula) can contain hundreds or even thousands of individuals. An important predator regulating rodent populations in grassland ecosystems.

children.name: Crotalus ruber
children.description: A large, reddish-coloured rattlesnake found in the chaparral and coastal sage scrub of southern California and the Baja California peninsula. Less aggressive than the Western Diamondback; bites are medically significant but rarely fatal with treatment. Listed as Near Threatened due to urban expansion.

children.name: Crotalus cerastes
children.description: A desert rattlesnake with a unique sidewinding locomotion; moves sideways across loose sand.

children.name: Crotalus lepidus
children.description: A beautiful grey-green rattlesnake from southwestern US and Mexico; prefers rocky slopes.

children.name: Crotalus ornatus
children.description: Formerly a subspecies of C. molossus; recent genetic studies elevated it to full species.

children.description: A Mexican species from the Pacific slope; grey with diamond-shaped blotches.

children.name: Sistrurus
children.description: Pygmy rattlesnakes and massasaugas — smaller rattlesnakes distinguished from Crotalus by having large, plate-like scales on the top of the head rather than the many small scales of their larger relatives. Sistrurus contains only two species, both found in North America. Their rattles are smaller and produce a faint buzzing sound rather than the loud warning rattle of large Crotalus. Despite their smaller size, envenomations require medical attention.

children.name: Sistrurus catenatus
children.description: A small, secretive rattlesnake of wetlands, wet prairies, and adjacent uplands in the Great Lakes region and upper Midwest. Listed as Threatened under the U.S. Endangered Species Act; populations have declined severely due to wetland drainage and habitat fragmentation. One of the rarest rattlesnakes in North America.

children.name: Sistrurus miliarius
children.description: North America's smallest rattlesnake, rarely exceeding 50 cm. Found across the southeastern United States from North Carolina to eastern Texas in pine flatwoods, sandhills, and mixed woodlands. Its tiny rattle produces a faint buzzing sound often mistaken for an insect. Despite small size, bites cause significant local tissue damage.

children.name: Bothrops
children.description: Lancehead pit vipers of Central and South America — one of the most medically significant genera of venomous snakes in the New World. Bothrops species are responsible for the majority of snakebite deaths in Latin America. They are fast-growing, aggressive when disturbed, and often found in agricultural settings where they encounter humans. The Fer-de-Lance is the most feared, and the Terciopelo (*B. asper*) is responsible for more human deaths in Costa Rica than all other venomous animals combined.

children.name: Bothrops atrox
children.description: One of the main causes of snakebite mortality in Amazonian South America. Nocturnal and terrestrial; patterned in browns and greys that blend perfectly with forest leaf litter. Females give birth to large litters of up to 80 neonates.

children.name: Bothrops insularis
children.description: Endemic to Ilha da Queimada Grande off São Paulo — 'Snake Island'. Population estimated at 2,000–4,000 individuals on a 43-hectare island. Access is restricted to researchers; the island is closed to the public. Critically Endangered.

children.name: Bothrops asper
children.description: The most medically important snake in Central America, responsible for the majority of serious snakebite cases from Mexico to Colombia. Highly variable in pattern and found in habitats from sea level to 2 500 m. A large, fast-growing species; females can reach over 2 m and produce litters of up to 90 neonates.

children.name: Bothrops jararaca
children.description: The primary cause of snakebite mortality in southeastern Brazil. Of enormous medical importance: research into its bradykinin-potentiating peptides led directly to the development of ACE inhibitor drugs (captopril), transforming the treatment of hypertension. A key example of how venom research translates to human medicine.

children.name: Bothrops alternatus
children.description: A large, robust lancehead of Argentina, Uruguay, Paraguay, and southern Brazil, typically found in grasslands and gallery forests near rivers and wetlands. Recognised by its distinctive crossband pattern forming half-moon shapes along the sides. An important cause of snakebite in the southern cone of South America.

children.name: Bothrops moojeni
children.description: A large aggressive lancehead from central and southeastern Brazil; named after the Moojen collector.

children.name: Bothrops neuwidi
children.description: A complex of cryptic species from eastern and southern South America.

children.name: Bothrops eritromelas
children.description: A small Bothrops from the Atlantic Forest of Brazil; black tail tip used as caudal lure.

children.name: Bothrops isabelae
children.description: A recently described lancehead from the Brazilian Atlantic Forest.

children.name: Bothrops saoleopoldensis
children.description: A species from southern Brazil; cryptic coloration with dark diamond pattern.

children.name: Bothrops urasti
children.description: A rare lancehead from the Tumucumaque region of the Amazon.

children.name: Bothrops sanctacruzis
children.description: A small species from the Bolivian Amazon; grey-brown with darker hourglass markings.

children.name: Bothriechis
children.description: Palm pit vipers — a genus of slender, arboreal pit vipers of Central America and the northern Andes, named for their association with palms and other tree vegetation. Bothriechis species are among the most strikingly coloured pit vipers, including the jewel-like B. schlegelii. All species have a strongly prehensile tail adapted for gripping branches. Though rarely fatal, envenomations cause severe local tissue destruction that can result in permanent disability.

children.name: Bothriechis schlegelii
children.description: One of the most colourful and well-known arboreal pit vipers, named for the modified supraocular scales that resemble eyelashes. Extraordinarily colour-polymorphic — individuals may be yellow, green, red, brown, or speckled. Ranges from Mexico to Ecuador and Venezuela.

children.name: Bothriechis lateralis
children.description: A vividly green arboreal pit viper of montane cloud forests of Costa Rica and western Panama. Distinguished by a pale lateral stripe and yellow tail tip used for caudal luring. Its habitat specificity to cloud forest makes it vulnerable to climate change-driven upslope habitat shifts.

children.name: Bothriechis nigroviridis
children.description: A montane cloud forest specialist found in the highlands of Costa Rica and Panama, typically at elevations of 1 500–2 000 m. Its vivid green scales are speckled with black, providing excellent camouflage in lichen-covered branches. Poorly studied; natural history data are limited.

children.name: Agkistrodon
children.description: Copperheads and cottonmouths — pit vipers of North America and Central America. Agkistrodon are among the most familiar venomous snakes of eastern North America and are responsible for large numbers of snakebite cases each year. The Water Moccasin (Cottonmouth) is the world's only semiaquatic viper. Copperhead envenomations are the most frequent venomous snakebite in the eastern USA but are rarely fatal.

children.name: Agkistrodon contortrix
children.description: The most common cause of venomous snakebite in the United States, responsible for thousands of bites annually, though fatalities are extremely rare. Distinctive hourglass-shaped crossbands on a copper-coloured body make it easily identified. Frequently found in suburban areas where woodland edges and rockpiles persist.

children.name: Agkistrodon piscivorus
children.description: The world's only semiaquatic viper, found in swamps, lakes, rivers, and marshes of the southeastern United States. The famous 'cotton-mouth' threat display — opening the jaws wide to expose the white interior — is a defensive warning. Highly potent venom causes severe local tissue destruction.

children.name: Trimeresurus
children.description: Asian green pit vipers — a large and diverse genus of arboreal and semi-arboreal pit vipers of South and Southeast Asia. Most species are a vivid lime-green with a white or red lateral stripe. Males of many species display spectacular red or orange eyes. Trimeresurus species are responsible for many snakebite cases across rural Asia; their cytotoxic and haemotoxic venom causes significant local tissue damage. The genus contains over 40 species following recent molecular revisions that elevated several subgenera.

children.name: Trimeresurus albolabris
children.description: One of the most frequently encountered venomous snakes across Southeast Asia, found from India to southern China and throughout Indochina in secondary forest, plantations, and gardens. Males are bright green with a white lateral stripe; females are larger and heavier-bodied. Responsible for significant morbidity in rural communities.

children.name: Trimeresurus stejnegeri
children.description: A widespread arboreal pit viper of southern China, Taiwan, and northern Indochina. Male with a vivid red and white lateral stripe, female plain green. Common in highland forests and bamboo groves; frequently encountered on hiking trails in Taiwan and southern China. Named after herpetologist Leonhard Stejneger.

children.name: Trimeresurus purpureomaculatus
children.description: A distinctive grey-purple pit viper of coastal mangroves and adjacent lowland forests from Bangladesh through the Malay Peninsula to the Andaman Islands. Unlike its green congeners, its dark colouration is suited to the dark mangrove environment. Responsible for bites among shrimp and crab harvesters working in mangrove areas.

children.name: Trimeresurus gramineus
children.description: The first Asian pit viper to receive a formal scientific description (by Shaw in 1802), found in forests and plantations of peninsular India and Sri Lanka. Arboreal and nocturnal; frequently found coiled on low vegetation or tea bushes in plantation estates. Responsible for a notable number of bites in tea-growing regions.

children.name: Trimeresurus hageni
children.description: A large green pit viper from Sumatra and Borneo; named after the collector.

children.name: Trimeresurus popeorum
children.description: Widespread from northeastern India to Vietnam; bright green with a red tail.

children.name: Protobothrops
children.description: Habu and large Asian pit vipers — a genus of large, terrestrial to semi-arboreal pit vipers found from Japan through China and the Himalayan foothills to Southeast Asia. Several species are of major medical significance; the Habu (P. flavoviridis) of the Ryukyu Islands is the subject of an ongoing control programme. Protobothrops were formerly included in Trimeresurus but molecular work confirmed them as a distinct early-diverging lineage within the Asian pit vipers.

children.name: Protobothrops flavoviridis
children.description: The feared habu of the Ryukyu Islands of Japan, responsible for hundreds of bites annually on Okinawa and neighbouring islands. Aggressively defensive and capable of striking with great speed. Subject to decades of control efforts including bounties; a mongoose introduction programme in 1910 has had limited effect and created new ecological problems.

children.name: Protobothrops mucrosquamatus
children.description: A widely distributed pit viper of southern China, Taiwan, Myanmar, and northern Indochina, found in a wide range of habitats from lowland farmland to montane forest. One of the most important causes of snakebite on Taiwan. Often found near human habitation; a versatile habitat generalist.
